1:
On Luke 20:1 — “on one of those days, as he taught the people in the temple, and preached the gospel, the chief priests and the scribes came upon him with the elders” — the witnesses mark the rulers’ challenge to the teaching Christ.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the hostility behind the coming. Bede: when they ask “by what authority,” they “doubt concerning the power of God, and wish it to be understood that of the devil He does this” — “most plainly do they deny the Son of God.” Theophylact and Eusebius note the Lord will answer by John the Baptist, exposing that they had “always rebelled against the Holy Spirit.” The grammatical-historical Robertson dates it “the last day of the temple teaching (Tuesday),” the verb (epestesan) meaning they “stood up against him” (Matthew 21:23; Mark 11:27).

The Puritan Henry marks the scene: “Christ was a preacher of his own gospel… He not only purchased the salvation for us, but published it.”

So the witnesses open the long day of controversy that fills the chapter. As the Lord teaches and preaches in the temple, the assembled authorities — chief priests, scribes, elders, the very Sanhedrin — confront him over his right to act. The witnesses note the malice the fathers discern: the question of authority is no honest inquiry but a denial, hinting that his works are of the devil rather than God. Yet they come to one who is the Lord of the temple, openly proclaiming the gospel to the people; and his very preaching, Henry observes, is the act of the Saviour publishing the salvation he came to purchase. The verse thus sets the stage for the series of traps — on authority, tribute, resurrection, and David’s Son — by which the rulers will seek to discredit him, and which he will turn, one by one, to their confusion.

2:
On Luke 20:2 — “Tell us, by what authority doest thou these things? or who is he that gave thee this authority?” — the witnesses mark the demand of the Sanhedrin.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the form and force. Ellicott: “the form of the question is nearly identical in the three Gospels.” Robertson observes Luke adds “tell us” (eipon hemin), a peremptory imperative. Gill weighs whether “these things” means his preaching or his cleansing of the temple and miracles — likely the whole of his recent acts of Messianic authority.

The verse gives the rulers’ double question. They demand to know both the kind of authority by which Christ acts — prophetic? royal? divine? — and its source: who commissioned him. The challenge is framed to force him either to a claim they can call blasphemy or to a retreat that discredits him.

So the witnesses read the question as a trap dressed as inquiry. The Sanhedrin, the official guardians of religion, demand Christ’s credentials, as though he were an upstart acting without warrant. The witnesses note the cunning: if he claims divine authority outright, they will charge him; if he cannot name a human commission, they will dismiss him. They mark that “these things” embraces all his bold acts — the cleansing of the temple, the teaching, the works — deeds that plainly assumed a right belonging to the Lord of the temple alone. The very framing betrays their refusal to read the evidence already before them; for the authority they question had been attested by his works and his word. The verse thus opens the contest of wits in which the Lord, instead of answering directly, will pose a counter-question that exposes the questioners’ own bad faith.

So the witnesses read the question as a trap dressed as inquiry. The Sanhedrin demand Christ’s credentials, as though he were an upstart acting without warrant. The witnesses note the cunning: a divine claim they will call blasphemy, a missing human commission they will use to dismiss him. They mark that “these things” embraces all his bold acts — the cleansing of the temple, the teaching, the works — deeds that plainly assumed a right belonging to the Lord of the temple alone, deeds whose authority his word and works had already attested.
3:
On Luke 20:3 — “And he answered and said unto them, I will also ask you one thing; and answer me” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s counter-question.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes “one thing” is literally “one word” (logon), as in Mark 11:29; Matthew 21:24. Gill marks that Jesus “replied to them” not with a direct answer but with a question of his own.

The verse shows the Lord’s method of meeting the trap. He does not refuse to answer, nor walk into their snare; he poses a single counter-question, on the terms of which the whole matter will turn. His “answer me” presses them to commit themselves first.

So the witnesses admire the wisdom of the Lord’s reply. The rulers had hoped to force him onto dangerous ground; he turns the burden back on them with one searching question. The witnesses note that this is no evasion but a just probe: the answer to their question is bound up in the answer to his, for to acknowledge John’s authority would be to acknowledge Christ’s. By requiring them to answer first, he exposes whether they ask in good faith or in malice. The verse thus shows the Lord neither cornered nor cowed before the assembled Sanhedrin, but in calm command of the exchange, ready to make the questioners reveal, by their own reply, the dishonesty of their challenge — and so to silence them without ever stating the claim they meant to use against him.

So the witnesses admire the wisdom of the Lord’s reply. The rulers hoped to force him onto dangerous ground; he turns the burden back with one searching question. By requiring them to answer first, he exposes whether they ask in good faith or in malice — in calm command of the exchange before the assembled Sanhedrin. The verse thus shows the Lord turning a hostile challenge into an occasion of self-disclosure for his enemies, requiring them to weigh, in their own answer, the very evidence they had refused to read.
4:
On Luke 20:4 — “The baptism of John, was it from heaven, or of men?” — the witnesses mark the question that decides the matter.

The voice here is the Reformed Gill, who reads the point: John’s baptism “was a new ordinance, and John must have his authority for administering it either from God, or from men; and Christ is desirous to know from which he derived it in their opinion” — “suggesting, that by the same authority John… came baptizing, he himself came preaching.”

The verse gives the Lord’s decisive counter-question. He fixes on John the Baptist, whose ministry the people revered and who had borne witness to Christ: was John’s baptism a divine commission or a human invention? On their answer hangs the answer to their own demand.

So the witnesses see the masterstroke of the Lord’s reply. John’s authority and Christ’s stand or fall together, for John had pointed to Jesus as the Coming One; to confess John from heaven is to confess the One John announced. The witnesses note the inescapable bind the question creates: the rulers had never owned John’s divine commission, yet dared not deny it before a people who held John a prophet. The Lord thus answers their question about his authority by appealing to a witness they could neither honestly accept nor safely reject. The verse shows the wisdom that confounds the crafty: a single question, drawn from the recent and undeniable ministry of John, that lays bare the rulers’ insincerity and makes their own evasion the answer to their challenge.

5:
On Luke 20:5 — “And they reasoned with themselves, saying, If we shall say, From heaven; he will say, Why then believed ye him not?” — the witnesses mark the rulers’ private debate.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the verb “reasoned with themselves” (synelogisanto, “to bring together accounts”), an old word only here in the New Testament; Mark and Matthew have the kindred “they debated.” Gill: they “took counsel together, and debated the matter among themselves.”

The verse catches the Sanhedrin in their dilemma. They confer secretly, weighing the cost of each answer. To confess John’s baptism from heaven would convict them of unbelief, for they had not received his testimony; the Lord would at once ask why they had not believed him — and so, by implication, why they would not believe the One John proclaimed.

So the witnesses watch the questioners become the questioned. The rulers, who came to entrap, now huddle in anxious calculation, caught on the horns of the Lord’s dilemma. The witnesses note the revealing nature of their reasoning: they weigh not what is true but what is safe — not “was John from heaven?” but “what answer will least harm us?” This very calculation lays bare their bad faith, for men honestly seeking truth do not deliberate how to avoid it. The first horn, “From heaven,” they dare not take, for it would expose and condemn their own unbelief. The verse thus shows the Lord’s wisdom working: a single question has reduced the official guardians of religion to a frightened conspiracy, more concerned for their standing than for the truth they were charged to teach.

The first horn they dare not take, for it would condemn their own unbelief.
6:
On Luke 20:6 — “But and if we say, Of men; all the people will stone us: for they be persuaded that John was a prophet” — the witnesses mark the second horn of the dilemma.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the vividness. Ellicott: Luke gives “the more vivid utterance… all the people will stone us,” where the others have “we fear the people”; he points to the readiness of Jerusalem’s crowds for such violence (John 8:59; Acts 7:58). Robertson notes “will stone us” (katalithasei, “overwhelm us with stones,” only here in the New Testament) and the “settled state of persuasion” that John was a prophet (Matthew 14:5).

The verse gives the other danger. To call John’s baptism “of men” would enrage the people, who firmly held John a prophet; the rulers fear being stoned. Caught between conviction they will not own and a crowd they dare not provoke, they have no safe answer.

So the witnesses see the trap close upon the trappers. The first horn convicts them of unbelief; the second exposes them to the fury of the people who revered John. The witnesses note the irony that the Sanhedrin, charged to lead the people in the truth, are governed instead by fear of the people’s violence — their answer dictated not by what is right but by what is safe. They mark, with Ellicott, how ready the Jerusalem crowd was to take up stones, a readiness later turned on Christ himself and on Stephen. The verse thus completes the dilemma: the men who demanded Christ’s credentials are themselves unable to render a verdict on John’s, paralysed between a conviction they suppress and a mob they fear. Their cowardice is now fully exposed, and their pretended authority to judge Christ is shown to be hollow.

So the witnesses see the trap close upon the trappers. The first horn convicts them of unbelief; the second exposes them to the fury of the people who revered John. The witnesses note the irony that the Sanhedrin, charged to lead the people in truth, are governed instead by fear of the people’s violence — their answer dictated by what is safe, not what is right. They mark, with Ellicott, how ready the Jerusalem crowd was to take up stones, a readiness later turned on Christ and on Stephen. Their pretended authority to judge Christ is shown hollow.
7:
On Luke 20:7 — “And they answered, that they could not tell whence it was” — the witnesses mark the rulers’ evasive non-answer.

The voices here are brief. The Reformed Gill names it plainly: “in this, no doubt, they told an untruth… they chose rather to sacrifice their consciences than their interest, and pretend ignorance rather than profess the truth.” The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the indirect assertion with the negative (me eidenai), “that they knew not.”

The verse gives their craven reply. Unwilling to confess John from heaven and afraid to call him of men, they take refuge in a feigned ignorance — “we cannot tell.” The professed teachers of Israel plead they cannot judge a matter every common man had judged.

So the witnesses read the rulers’ answer as a self-condemning falsehood. They could tell, and did know; their “we cannot tell” is a lie told to escape the trap of their own making. The witnesses note, with Gill, the moral collapse it reveals: men who would sacrifice their consciences sooner than their interest, preferring a pretended ignorance to an honest confession that would cost them. The very office that demanded Christ’s credentials now abdicates its competence to judge the plainest of cases. The verse thus completes the exposure: the Sanhedrin, asked one question, is reduced to a public falsehood, forfeiting by its evasion any claim to sit in judgment on the authority of Jesus. Their refusal to answer is itself the answer — a confession that they will not own the truth they know.

The verse thus leaves the rulers self-convicted, their feigned ignorance a public confession that they would not own a truth the plainest of the people had already owned.
8:
On Luke 20:8 — “And Jesus said unto them, Neither tell I you by what authority I do these things” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s just refusal.

The voice here is the Reformed Gill: “since they would not give him a direct answer to his question,” he in turn declines to answer theirs.

The verse closes the first contest. Because the rulers would not answer the question on which the matter justly turned, the Lord declines to declare his authority to them. Their bad faith has forfeited their right to an answer.

So the witnesses see the Lord’s refusal as righteous, not evasive. He does not withhold the truth from honest seekers but from men who have just proved, by their feigned ignorance, that they ask in malice. The witnesses note the justice of it: they would not judge John’s authority, though they knew; therefore they shall not be told Christ’s, which the same evidence would establish. To men who suppress known truth, more truth is not owed. Yet the witnesses observe that the Lord has in fact answered them, indirectly and unanswerably: the authority of John and the authority of Jesus are one, and those who will not confess the first have no claim to hear the second declared. The verse thus ends the exchange with the questioners silenced and self-condemned, and the Lord’s authority left standing, attested by the very witness they dared not deny — before he turns, in the parable to follow, to pronounce their doom.

So the witnesses see the Lord’s refusal as righteous, not evasive. He withholds the truth not from honest seekers but from men who have just proved, by their feigned ignorance, that they ask in malice. The witnesses note the justice of it: they would not judge John’s authority, though they knew; therefore they shall not be told Christ’s, which the same evidence would establish. Yet he has answered them unanswerably — John’s authority and Christ’s are one — before turning, in the parable to follow, to pronounce their doom. The verse thus shows the Lord declining to cast pearls before men who had just trampled the light they had, leaving them with an answer they could not refute and a judgment they had earned.
9:
On Luke 20:9 — “A certain man planted a vineyard, and let it forth to husbandmen, and went into a far country for a long time” — the witnesses open the parable of the wicked husbandmen.

The Latin and Eastern fathers expound the allegory. Eusebius: Christ “put forth a parable foretelling… the things they were about to do to Him.” Ambrose, citing Isaiah 5:1: the “vineyard of the Lord… is the house of Israel,” planted by God himself. Cyril reads the “far country” as God withdrawing his visible presence after Sinai, yet still sending prophets. Chrysostom: it was “not accidentally but… the divine dispensation that Christ came after the prophets.” The grammatical-historical Robertson notes “for a long time” (chronous hikanous), which Wesley reads as the long span from Canaan to Christ.

The Puritan Henry draws the point against the rulers: “by questioning his authority they forfeited their own… Their disowning the lord of their vineyard was a defeasance of their lease.”

So the witnesses read the parable as the Lord’s answer to the rulers who challenged him — a story that foretells their crime and their judgment. The vineyard is Israel, planted and hedged by God (Isaiah 5:1–7); the husbandmen are the leaders entrusted with it; the owner’s long absence is God’s patience through the ages, never leaving his people without prophets. The witnesses note the fathers’ unanimous reading: every detail traces the history of Israel’s stewardship and rebellion. Spoken to the very men who would shortly kill the Heir, the parable is both prophecy and indictment. The verse thus opens a story in which the rulers will hear their own past, present, and doom rehearsed — the long-suffering of the God who planted the vineyard set against the long ingratitude of those to whom he let it out.

So the witnesses read the parable as the Lord’s answer to the rulers who challenged him — a story that foretells their crime and their judgment. The vineyard is Israel, planted and hedged by God (Isa 5:1-7); the husbandmen are the leaders entrusted with it; the owner’s long absence is God’s patience through the ages, never leaving his people without prophets. Spoken to the very men who would shortly kill the Heir, the parable is both prophecy and indictment — the long-suffering of God set against the long ingratitude of those to whom he let out the vineyard.
10:
On Luke 20:10 — “And at the season he sent a servant to the husbandmen, that they should give him of the fruit… but the husbandmen beat him, and sent him away empty” — the witnesses mark the first servant rejected.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the detail. Robertson: “at the season” is the definite time for fruit (Matthew 21:34); “that they should give” expresses purpose. Ellicott marks “beat him, and sent him away empty,” agreeing almost verbally with Mark. The Eastern Theophylact reads the “fruit” God seeks: “not the whole… but part only… what does God gain from us, but His own knowledge, which is also our profit.”

The verse begins the catalogue of rejected messengers. At harvest the owner sends for his due; the husbandmen, instead of rendering fruit, beat his servant and send him away with nothing. The first of God’s prophets is met with violence and contempt.

So the witnesses read in the beaten servant the pattern of Israel’s treatment of the prophets. God, having planted and waited, sends at the proper season to receive the fruit of righteousness owed him; the stewards answer with abuse. The witnesses note the fathers’ gloss on the “fruit”: God seeks not gain for himself, for he needs nothing, but the people’s own obedience and knowledge of him, which is their profit. The servant sent “empty” away is each prophet whose call to repentance was scorned and whose person was maltreated. The verse thus begins to fill in the long history the parable rehearses — a succession of messengers sent in patience and returned in shame — building toward the climactic sending of the Son, and exposing the settled ingratitude of those who held the vineyard yet would yield no fruit to its Lord.

God, having planted and waited, sends at the proper season to receive the fruit of righteousness owed him; the stewards answer with abuse. 11:
On Luke 20:11 — “And again he sent another servant: and they beat him also, and entreated him shamefully, and sent him away empty” — the witnesses mark the second servant abused.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the Hebraism “he added to send another” (prosetheto pempsai), repeated in verse 12. Ellicott marks “the emphatic repetition” of “sent him away empty” as “peculiar to St. Luke.” Gill reads it of a later “set of prophets… before the Babylonish captivity.”

The verse repeats the wrong with a second servant, treated yet more shamefully. Again the owner sends, again the husbandmen beat and insult his messenger and send him off empty-handed. The pattern of rejection deepens.

So the witnesses see the parable trace the lengthening record of Israel’s rebellion. Servant after servant — prophet after prophet, age after age — is sent in the owner’s patience and returned in shame, the insults mounting. The witnesses note Luke’s emphatic “sent him away empty,” underscoring that God’s repeated overtures met with nothing but contempt. The repetition is not mere narrative filling but the very point: the long-suffering of God answered by the long ingratitude of men. The witnesses mark how the parable mirrors the prophets’ own complaint — that God rose early and sent his servants, and they were beaten, mocked, and slain. The verse thus builds the indictment toward its climax: a vineyard so often visited, so persistently abused, that nothing remains but the sending of the Son — and beyond that, the reckoning the husbandmen have stored up for themselves.

So the witnesses see the parable trace the lengthening record of Israel’s rebellion. Servant after servant — prophet after prophet, age after age — is sent in the owner’s patience and returned in shame, the insults mounting. The witnesses note Luke’s emphatic “sent him away empty,” underscoring that God’s repeated overtures met only contempt. The repetition is the very point: the long-suffering of God answered by the long ingratitude of men, mirroring the prophets’ own complaint that God rose early and sent his servants, and they were beaten and slain.
12:
On Luke 20:12 — “And again he sent a third: and they wounded him also, and cast him out” — the witnesses mark the third servant wounded.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical commentators note Luke’s precise verb. Ellicott: “they wounded him” is “peculiar to St. Luke, and has a characteristic half-surgical ring” (used again in Acts 19:16); Robertson confirms traumatisantes, from “a wound.” Gill places it “after the return… from captivity… between that time and the coming of Christ.”

The verse marks the escalation to bloodshed. The third servant is not merely beaten but wounded and cast out — the abuse rising from insult to injury. The husbandmen grow bolder in their defiance.

So the witnesses see the wrong intensify toward its climax. Where the first was beaten and the second shamefully entreated, the third is wounded — the violence deepening with each sending. The witnesses note the physician’s exact word that Luke chooses for the wounding, a touch of his characteristic precision. The casting-out anticipates the fate of the Son, who too will be cast out of the vineyard and slain. The verse thus shows the long patience of God meeting an ever-hardening contempt: the more he sends, the worse his servants are used, until the measure of the husbandmen’s guilt is nearly full. It prepares the owner’s last and greatest venture — the sending of his beloved Son — against the dark background of a vineyard that has wounded and rejected every messenger of its Lord, and so heaps up the judgment soon to fall.

The witnesses note the physician’s exact word that Luke chooses for the wounding. The casting-out anticipates the fate of the Son, who too will be cast out of the vineyard and slain. The more God sends, the worse his servants are used, until the measure of the husbandmen’s guilt is nearly full — and the owner’s last venture, the beloved Son, is prepared against this dark background.
13:
On Luke 20:13 — “Then said the lord of the vineyard, What shall I do? I will send my beloved son: it may be they will reverence him when they see him” — the witnesses mark the sending of the Son.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the features peculiar to Luke. Robertson: “What shall I do?” and “it may be” (isos, “perhaps,” only here in the New Testament) are details “only in Luke.” Ellicott: the implied “doubt… is a feature peculiar to St. Luke’s report.” All three Gospels have “they will reverence him.”

The verse marks the owner’s last resort and the dignity of the one sent. Having spent his servants in vain, he resolves to send his “beloved son” — one of another rank entirely — in the hope that the husbandmen will reverence him. The escalation of messengers reaches its summit in the Son.

So the witnesses read in the sending of the beloved Son the heart of the parable and of the gospel. The owner, after long patience with abused servants, makes his supreme appeal: he sends not another prophet but his own Son, beloved and of his own nature — a clear self-disclosure by Christ of who he is, the Son sent last of all. The witnesses note the touching “it may be they will reverence him,” an accommodation to human speech that expresses God’s yearning over even these rebels, his desire that they repent. They mark that the very distinction between the “servants” and the “Son” sets Christ infinitely above the prophets. The verse thus reveals both the immeasurable grace of God, who sends his dearest in a final overture, and the Lord’s own consciousness of his unique Sonship — and prepares the terrible answer the husbandmen will give to this last and best of messengers.

So the witnesses read in the sending of the beloved Son the heart of the parable and of the gospel. The owner, after long patience with abused servants, makes his supreme appeal: not another prophet but his own Son, beloved and of his own nature — Christ’s self-disclosure as the Son sent last of all. The witnesses note the touching “it may be they will reverence him,” an accommodation to human speech expressing God’s yearning over even these rebels. The very distinction between servants and Son sets Christ infinitely above the prophets.
14:
On Luke 20:14 — “But when the husbandmen saw him, they reasoned among themselves, saying, This is the heir: come, let us kill him, that the inheritance may be ours” — the witnesses mark the murderous design.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the heir and the plot. Barnes points to the Son as “the heir” (Psalm 2:8; Hebrews 1:2) and the resolve “let us kill him” (Acts 2:23). Robertson notes Luke’s “that the inheritance may become ours,” where Matthew has “let us get” and Mark “it will be ours.” Gill reads it of the rulers who saw Christ “in human nature… and observed the miracles done by him.”

The verse lays bare the husbandmen’s reasoning. Recognising the heir, they plot his death, imagining that by killing him they will seize the inheritance for themselves. Their crime is committed with open eyes, against one they know to be the son.

So the witnesses see the parable expose the rulers’ guilt as deliberate, not ignorant. The husbandmen do not mistake the heir; they know him, and for that very reason resolve to kill him — their motive the grasping wish to possess the vineyard without its lord. The witnesses note the dreadful self-portrait the Lord paints of his enemies, who within days will do exactly this, delivering the Son of God to death. They mark the irony of their reasoning: by killing the heir they think to keep the inheritance, when in truth his death will be the occasion of their own dispossession. The verse thus reveals the heart of the rejection of Christ — not a failure to recognise him, but a refusal to render God his due, a covetous determination to be lords of what is God’s. The crime is knowing, and therefore the judgment, in the next verses, will be just.

15:
On Luke 20:15 — “So they cast him out of the vineyard, and killed him. What therefore shall the lord of the vineyard do unto them?” — the witnesses mark the murder of the Son and the question of judgment.

The voice here is the Reformed Gill, who traces the fulfilment: they “rejected him as the Messiah… delivered him to the Gentiles… and at last had him without their city, and put him to death” — the casting-out of the vineyard answering to Christ crucified outside Jerusalem.

The verse brings the parable to its crime and turns to its sequel. The husbandmen cast the son out of the vineyard and kill him; and the Lord puts the pointed question — what will the owner now do to such men? The hearers are made to pronounce on a case that is their own.

So the witnesses read in the murdered son the plainest foretelling of the cross. Gill marks the exactness: the Son cast out of the vineyard and slain answers to Christ led outside the city and crucified, rejected by his own and delivered to the Gentiles. The witnesses note the rhetorical turn — “What shall the lord do?” — by which the Lord draws his hearers to judge the husbandmen, and so, unwittingly, themselves. The parable has now declared, before the event, both the crime the rulers are about to commit and the certainty of its requital. The verse thus stands at the hinge between the deed and its doom: the Son’s death foretold in figure, and the owner’s response demanded — that the guilty may hear their sentence from their own lips, and that the justice of God against those who reject his Son may be made undeniable.

So the witnesses read in the murdered son the plainest foretelling of the cross. Gill marks the exactness: the Son cast out of the vineyard and slain answers to Christ led outside the city and crucified, rejected by his own and delivered to the Gentiles.
