16:
On Luke 20:16 — “He shall come and destroy these husbandmen, and shall give the vineyard to others. And when they heard it, they said, God forbid” — the witnesses mark the sentence and the hearers’ recoil.

The grammatical-historical commentators note who speaks and the recoil. Ellicott: Luke, with Mark, puts “he shall come and destroy” “into our Lord’s lips,” not the bystanders’ (as Matthew); “God forbid” renders me genoito, though “the name of God does not appear in the original.” Robertson calls it “the pious protest of the defeated members of the Sanhedrin who began to see the turn of the parable against themselves.” Gill notes the destruction “had its accomplishment at the destruction of Jerusalem” (Nehemiah 9:36).

The verse pronounces the doom and records the rulers’ horror. The owner will destroy the murderous husbandmen and give the vineyard to others; and the hearers, perceiving the parable’s aim, cry out “God forbid.” The sentence is spoken, and its targets flinch.

So the witnesses read the parable’s climax as both judgment and prophecy. The husbandmen’s destruction foretells the ruin of Jerusalem and the rejected leaders; the giving of the vineyard to others foretells the calling of the Gentiles and the new stewardship of the gospel. The witnesses note the rulers’ recoil — “God forbid” — the protest of men who now see themselves in the wicked husbandmen and cannot bear the application, yet will not repent of the crime the parable describes. Wesley marks that they could allow that wretches deserved destruction but “could not bear that this should be applied to themselves.” The verse thus shows the word of Christ finding its mark: the guilty have heard their own doom, recognised it, and shrunk from it — not in repentance but in dismay — while the Lord proceeds to seal the sentence with Scripture in the verses that follow.

So the witnesses read the parable’s climax as both judgment and prophecy. The husbandmen’s destruction foretells the ruin of Jerusalem and the rejected leaders; the giving of the vineyard to others foretells the calling of the Gentiles and the new stewardship of the gospel. The witnesses note the rulers’ recoil — “God forbid” — the protest of men who see themselves in the wicked husbandmen yet will not repent. Wesley marks that they could allow such wretches deserved destruction but could not bear it applied to themselves.
17:
On Luke 20:17 — “And he beheld them, and said, What is this then that is written, The stone which the builders rejected, the same is become the head of the corner?” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s appeal to the prophetic stone.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the gaze and the text. Ellicott and Robertson: “he beheld them” (emblepsas) is “a piercing glance,” not in Mark and Matthew, the fixed look of one applying the word to its hearers. The Scripture is Psalm 118:22, quoted in all three Synoptics. Gill: he looked “earnestly… signifying that verily so it would be.”

The verse turns from parable to Scripture. With a searching look, the Lord confronts the rulers with the psalm of the rejected stone made the cornerstone: the builders — they themselves — reject the stone, yet God exalts it to the head of the corner. The figure of the vineyard gives way to the figure of the building.

So the witnesses see the Lord seal the parable’s lesson with the authority of Scripture. The “builders” are Israel’s leaders, charged to build God’s house; the “stone” they reject is Christ himself; and the psalm declares that the very stone they cast aside God makes the chief cornerstone of his building. The witnesses note the piercing look that accompanies the word — the Lord fixing his gaze on the men who are even now rejecting him, that they may feel the application. The figure answers the parable: as the husbandmen killed the heir, so the builders reject the stone; and as the owner destroys the husbandmen, so God exalts the rejected stone over them. The verse thus turns the rulers’ own Scriptures against their unbelief, proclaiming that their rejection of Christ cannot defeat God’s purpose but only fulfil it — and prepares the warning of the stone’s twofold power in the verse to follow.

So the witnesses see the Lord seal the parable’s lesson with the authority of Scripture. The “builders” are Israel’s leaders; the “stone” they reject is Christ; and the psalm declares that the very stone they cast aside God makes the chief cornerstone of his building. The witnesses note the piercing look that accompanies the word, the Lord fixing his gaze on the men now rejecting him. Their rejection of Christ cannot defeat God’s purpose but only fulfil it — and prepares the warning of the stone’s twofold power to follow.
18:
On Luke 20:18 — “Whosoever shall fall upon that stone shall be broken; but on whomsoever it shall fall, it will grind him to powder” — the witnesses mark the twofold peril of the stone.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the imagery and the texts. Robertson: “shall be broken to pieces” (synthlasthesetai, to shatter) and “will scatter him as dust” (likmesei, to winnow, grind to powder), the verse found in all manuscripts of Luke though disputed in Matthew 21:44. Clarke points to the stone of Daniel 2:34. Gill distinguishes those who stumble at Christ in unbelief from those crushed in final judgment.

The verse declares the double danger of the rejected stone. To fall upon it — to stumble at Christ in offence and unbelief — is to be broken; to have it fall upon one — to meet Christ in judgment — is to be ground to powder. Either way, the stone the builders despised proves their ruin.

So the witnesses read the warning that crowns the figure of the stone. Christ rejected is not a stone men may safely set aside; he is a stone of stumbling now and a stone of crushing judgment hereafter. The witnesses note the two perils Gill distinguishes: the present breaking of those who stumble at Christ in unbelief, hurting themselves on the offence of the cross; and the final, irreparable destruction of those on whom the stone falls in judgment, recalling the stone of Daniel that smote the image and became a mountain. The verse thus turns the rulers’ rejection back upon themselves with terrible force: the heir they would cast out, the stone they would discard, is the very rock on which they break and beneath which they will be crushed. To refuse the cornerstone is not a neutral act but a fatal one; and the warning stands over every age that stumbles at the Christ whom God has exalted.

So the witnesses read the warning that crowns the figure of the stone. Christ rejected is not a stone men may safely set aside; he is a stone of stumbling now and a stone of crushing judgment hereafter. The witnesses note the two perils Gill distinguishes: the present breaking of those who stumble at Christ in unbelief, and the final, irreparable destruction of those on whom the stone falls in judgment, recalling Daniel’s stone that smote the image. To refuse the cornerstone is not a neutral act but a fatal one.
19:
On Luke 20:19 — “And the chief priests and the scribes the same hour sought to lay hands on him; and they feared the people: for they perceived that he had spoken this parable against them” — the witnesses mark the rulers’ rage and restraint.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the irony. Cyril: it became them, “perceiving that the parable was spoken of them, to depart from evil… but… they rather gather a fresh occasion for their crimes.” Bede: “by seeking to slay Him, they proved the truth of what He had said” — they are the wicked husbandmen, doing the very deed the parable foretold. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes “to lay hands on him” and “in that very hour,” their seizing checked only by fear of the people.

The verse shows the parable’s effect. The rulers, stung by its plain application, seek that very hour to seize Christ, restrained only by their fear of the crowd. They understood the parable, and answered it not with repentance but with murderous intent.

So the witnesses see the rulers confirm the parable in the act of resenting it. Convicted of being the wicked husbandmen, they respond by plotting to do exactly what the husbandmen did — to lay hands on the Son and kill him. The witnesses note, with Bede, the dreadful self-fulfilment: their very rage proves the truth of the charge, for they hasten to commit the crime the parable described. Cyril marks the tragedy that, warned so plainly, they did not turn from evil but redoubled it. Only the fear of the people stays their hands for the moment — not conscience, but calculation. The verse thus shows the hardening that follows resisted truth: the word that should have brought repentance brings only a fiercer hatred, and the men who heard their own doom pronounced set themselves the more determinedly to earn it. Their understanding of the parable becomes not a warning heeded but a grievance avenged.

So the witnesses see the rulers confirm the parable in the act of resenting it. Convicted of being the wicked husbandmen, they respond by plotting to do exactly what the husbandmen did — to seize and kill the Son. The witnesses note, with Bede, the dreadful self-fulfilment: their very rage proves the truth of the charge. Cyril marks the tragedy that, warned so plainly, they did not turn from evil but redoubled it. Only fear of the people stays their hands — not conscience, but calculation; the word that should have brought repentance brings a fiercer hatred.
20:
On Luke 20:20 — “And they watched him, and sent forth spies, which should feign themselves just men, that they might take hold of his words, so as to deliver him unto the power and authority of the governor” — the witnesses mark the plot of the spies.

The grammatical-historical commentators expose the machinery. Clarke: “watched him” (paratoresantes) is “insidiously watching”; the “spies” (enkathetous) are men “set in ambush… to spy, listen, catch.” Robertson notes the watching “with evil intent” (Luke 6:7). The Puritan Henry: the plot was “to deliver him unto the power… of the governor” — to make him a political offender.

The verse uncovers the next snare. Foiled in the open challenge, the rulers turn to subterfuge: hired spies, masked as conscientious inquirers, are sent to trap Christ in his words, that they may hand him over to the Roman governor as a seditious offender.

So the witnesses read the shift from confrontation to conspiracy. Unable to seize Christ openly for fear of the people, the rulers resort to ambush — men who feign righteousness to lower his guard and catch some incautious word. The witnesses note the deep hypocrisy the language exposes: “spies” who “feign themselves just men,” cloaking malice in the garb of piety. Their aim, Henry observes, is political — to entangle Christ with Rome, so that the governor’s power may do what their own fear of the crowd forbids. The verse thus reveals the lengths of the rulers’ enmity: having failed to discredit Christ’s authority and recoiled from his parable, they now plot to destroy him by deceit and by the arm of the state. It sets the stage for the question on tribute — a trap baited with flattery and aimed at the governor’s sword.

So the witnesses read the shift from confrontation to conspiracy. Unable to seize Christ openly for fear of the people, the rulers resort to ambush — men who feign righteousness to lower his guard and catch some incautious word. The witnesses note the deep hypocrisy the language exposes: “spies” who “feign themselves just men,” cloaking malice in piety. Their aim, Henry observes, is political — to entangle Christ with Rome, so the governor’s power may do what their fear of the crowd forbids. The stage is set for the question on tribute.
21:
On Luke 20:21 — “Master, we know that thou sayest and teachest rightly, neither acceptest thou the person of any, but teachest the way of God truly” — the witnesses mark the flattery that baits the trap.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the smooth approach. Robertson: these “spies” were “disciples of the Pharisees” with Herodians (Matthew 22:16; Mark 12:13), “full of palaver and flattery,” openly endorsing his teaching as part of their scheme. Ellicott notes “neither acceptest thou the person of any” recurs in Galatians 2:6. Wesley reads the artful balance: “thou speakest — in private, and teachest — in public.”

The verse opens the trap with elaborate compliment. The spies praise Christ’s honesty and impartiality — that he teaches God’s way truly and fears no man’s face — hoping by this flattery to provoke a bold, unguarded answer on the dangerous matter of tribute.

So the witnesses see in the flattery the cunning of the snare. The very words are true — Christ does teach rightly and regard no man’s person — but they are spoken in hypocrisy, to disarm him and draw out a saying they can use. The witnesses note the irony: the spies unwittingly testify to the integrity they hope to exploit, confessing that Christ will not flatter the powerful nor trim his speech to please. That very fearlessness, they calculate, will make him answer their tribute-question without the caution that would save him. The verse thus exposes the deceit that masks itself in praise: men who feign admiration for Christ’s truthfulness while plotting to entrap him by it. And it sets up the contrast to come — the Lord, who indeed regards no man’s person, will see through their craft and turn their own flattery to confusion.

So the witnesses see in the flattery the cunning of the snare. The very words are true — Christ does teach rightly and regard no man’s person — but they are spoken in hypocrisy, to disarm him and draw out a saying they can use. The witnesses note the irony: the spies unwittingly testify to the integrity they hope to exploit, confessing that Christ will not flatter the powerful. That very fearlessness, they calculate, will make him answer their tribute-question without saving caution. The Lord, who regards no man’s person, will see through their craft.
22:
On Luke 20:22 — “Is it lawful for us to give tribute unto Caesar, or no?” — the witnesses mark the loaded question.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the danger in the question. Robertson: “tribute” (phoron) is the annual land-tax, where Mark and Matthew have the Latin kenson; “the picture on the coin may have been that of Tiberius.” Gill marks the emphasis on “for us”: the pretended doubt was not whether Rome might tax, but whether Jews, God’s people, ought to pay a heathen ruler.

The verse springs the trap. The spies pose the explosive question of tribute to Caesar — a matter on which any answer seems fatal. To say “pay” would offend the people who chafed under Rome; to say “do not pay” would give Rome a charge of sedition.

So the witnesses see the carefully baited dilemma. The question of tribute was inflammatory: many Jews held it unlawful for God’s people to acknowledge a pagan overlord by paying his tax, while Rome treated refusal as rebellion. The witnesses note the emphasis Gill marks on “for us” — the spies frame it as a matter of religious principle, whether the covenant people may rightly submit to Caesar. Either horn seems to destroy Christ: a “yes” alienates the patriotic crowd whose favour shields him; a “no” arms the governor against him as a seditionist. The verse thus presents the most dangerous of the day’s traps, designed to force the Lord into either popular disgrace or capital charge — and prepares his famous answer, which will honour both the duty owed to Caesar and the higher duty owed to God, and so escape the snare entirely.

So the witnesses see the carefully baited dilemma. The question of tribute was inflammatory: many Jews held it unlawful for God’s people to acknowledge a pagan overlord, while Rome treated refusal as rebellion. The witnesses note the emphasis Gill marks on “for us” — framed as religious principle. Either horn seems to destroy Christ: a “yes” alienates the patriotic crowd whose favour shields him; a “no” arms the governor against him as a seditionist — the most dangerous of the day’s traps.
23:
On Luke 20:23 — “But he perceived their craftiness, and said unto them” — the witnesses mark the Lord seeing through the snare.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the discernment. Robertson: “perceived” (katanoesas, “to put the mind down on”); “craftiness” (panourgian), where Matthew has “wickedness” and Mark “hypocrisy” — “unscrupulous” cunning. Ellicott notes the noun is used four times by Paul (2 Corinthians 4:2; Ephesians 4:14). Gill: he “clearly saw that their view was” to charge him before the governor or expose him to the people.

The verse shows the Lord unmasking the plot before he answers. He perceives the craftiness beneath the flattery — the trap concealed in the question — and meets it with open eyes. The spies’ disguise does not deceive the one who reads hearts.

So the witnesses mark the Lord’s penetration of the snare. The spies had feigned sincerity and dressed their malice in compliment; but Christ, the discerner of thoughts, sees their craftiness at once. The witnesses note that he is not caught off guard by either the flattery or the dilemma: he knows both the question’s hidden intent and the questioners’ hidden hearts. This perception is itself a quiet testimony to who he is — the one before whom no disguise avails. The verse thus turns the encounter: where the spies thought to ensnare an unsuspecting teacher, they face one who has already seen through them. His coming answer will be no anxious threading of a dilemma but the calm word of one who masters the trap from above it. The verse assures us that the Lord’s reply springs not from cleverness under pressure but from a wisdom that read the plot before a word of it was answered.

So the witnesses mark the Lord’s penetration of the snare. The spies had feigned sincerity and dressed their malice in compliment; but Christ, the discerner of thoughts, sees their craftiness at once. The witnesses note that he is not caught off guard by either the flattery or the dilemma: he knows both the question’s hidden intent and the questioners’ hidden hearts. This perception is itself a quiet testimony to who he is — the one before whom no disguise avails. His coming answer springs not from cleverness under pressure but from a wisdom that read the plot before a word was answered.
24:
On Luke 20:24 — “Shew me a penny. Whose image and superscription hath it? They answered and said, Caesar’s” — the witnesses mark the coin that decides the question.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the coin: Gill and Wesley, “a Roman denarius… the money that was usually paid” in tribute, bearing Caesar’s image. They answer, of necessity, “Caesar’s.”

The verse turns the question by an object lesson. The Lord calls for the tribute-coin and asks whose image and inscription it bears; the spies must answer, “Caesar’s.” By their own admission the currency of the realm is the emperor’s.

So the witnesses see the Lord answer the trap with a question of his own, drawn from the coin in their hands. The denarius they used in daily trade bore Caesar’s image and name — the token of his rule, the very money in which the tax was paid. By making them name whose image it carried, the Lord leads them to supply the premise of his reply: that to use Caesar’s coin is already to acknowledge Caesar’s authority. The witnesses note the simplicity and force of the method — no abstract argument, but a plain fact held up before all. The verse thus prepares the great saying to follow: the coin stamped with Caesar’s image owes a debt to Caesar, even as man, stamped with God’s image, owes a debt to God. From their own lips, “Caesar’s,” the Lord draws the thread that will unravel their snare.

So the witnesses see the Lord answer the trap with a question of his own, drawn from the coin in their hands. The denarius they used in daily trade bore Caesar’s image and name — the token of his rule, the very money in which the tax was paid. By making them name whose image it carried, the Lord leads them to supply the premise of his reply: that to use Caesar’s coin is already to acknowledge Caesar’s authority. From their own lips, “Caesar’s,” he draws the thread that will unravel their snare. The verse thus shows the Lord meeting craft with simplicity, drawing from a coin what no argument could have wrung from his adversaries — their own acknowledgment of the authority they pretended to question.
25:
On Luke 20:25 — “Render therefore unto Caesar the things which be Caesar’s, and unto God the things which be God’s” — the witnesses mark the great saying that escapes the snare.

The grammatical-historical Barnes points to the duty of paying lawful taxes (Romans 13:7). The Eastern Origen draws the deeper sense from the verse’s context: “there are two images in man, one which he received from God… another from the enemy… through disobedience and sin” — as the coin bears Caesar’s image, so the duty to God belongs to the image of God in us. Gill: “give to the king what is the king’s… since they were under his government, and protected by him.”

The verse gives the Lord’s incomparable answer. Caesar’s coin bears Caesar’s image, so render Caesar his due; but man bears God’s image, so render God his due. The two duties are distinguished, neither denied; and the trap dissolves.

So the witnesses marvel at the wisdom that turns the snare into a charter of duty. The Lord neither denies the lawfulness of tribute — which would have been sedition — nor exalts Caesar above God — which would have offended the people; he distinguishes the spheres. To use Caesar’s currency and enjoy his order is to owe Caesar his tax; and that duty, far from clashing with religion, is part of it (Romans 13:7). But over and above all earthly allegiance stands the supreme claim of God, to whom we owe ourselves. The witnesses note Origen’s profound gloss: as the coin stamped with Caesar’s image is rendered to Caesar, so the soul stamped with God’s image must be rendered to God. The verse thus answers the question and transcends it, laying down the abiding principle of the two allegiances — honour to the magistrate in his sphere, and the whole heart to God in his — and so escapes the trap by rising above it.

So the witnesses marvel at the wisdom that turns the snare into a charter of duty. The Lord neither denies the lawfulness of tribute — which would have been sedition — nor exalts Caesar above God — which would have offended the people; he distinguishes the spheres. To use Caesar’s currency is to owe Caesar his tax (Rom 13:7); but over all earthly allegiance stands the supreme claim of God. Origen’s gloss is profound: as the coin stamped with Caesar’s image is rendered to Caesar, so the soul stamped with God’s image must be rendered to God.
26:
On Luke 20:26 — “And they could not take hold of his words before the people: and they marvelled at his answer, and held their peace” — the witnesses mark the spies’ defeat.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the completeness of it. Robertson: these “crack students had made an ignominious failure” and “could not… make a case for the surrender of Jesus to Pilate. He had slipped through” their net. Ellicott notes Luke is “fuller… in” recounting both the plot and its defeat. Gill: they got no “seditious… expressions” to use against him.

The verse records the rout of the spies. They cannot lay hold of his words before the people, as they had hoped; instead they marvel at his answer and fall silent. The trap baited with flattery has caught nothing.

So the witnesses see the second great trap fail as the first. The spies, sent to catch some incriminating word, find none; the Lord’s answer gave Rome no charge of sedition and the people no cause of offence, and left the questioners speechless with wonder. The witnesses note the irony of their marvelling: the very men who came to entrap are reduced to admiration, confounded by a wisdom they cannot gainsay. Their silence, like the Sanhedrin’s earlier, is the silence of defeat. The verse thus closes the tribute controversy as it closed the question of authority — with the adversaries baffled and the Lord’s word standing unanswerable before the people. It prepares the third assault, from the Sadducees on the resurrection, in a chapter that displays, trap after trap, the unconquerable wisdom of Christ, who turns every snare of his enemies to their confusion and his own vindication.

So the witnesses see the second great trap fail as the first. The spies, sent to catch some incriminating word, find none; the Lord’s answer gave Rome no charge of sedition and the people no cause of offence, and left the questioners speechless with wonder. The witnesses note the irony of their marvelling: the very men who came to entrap are reduced to admiration. Their silence, like the Sanhedrin’s earlier, is the silence of defeat — preparing the third assault, from the Sadducees on the resurrection.
27:
On Luke 20:27 — “Then came to him certain of the Sadducees, which deny that there is any resurrection” — the witnesses mark the third assault, from the deniers of the resurrection.

The Latin and Eastern fathers identify the sect and its error. Bede: of the two heresies, the Sadducees’ “name signified ‘righteous,’ claiming… that which they were not.” Origen: their heresy “not only denies the resurrection of the dead, but also believes the soul to die with the body,” and they came “watching to entrap our Saviour.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: after the rout of Pharisees and Herodians, the Sadducees “rally” with their “stock conundrum” (Matthew 22:23; Acts 23:8).

The verse introduces the new questioners. The Sadducees — the party that denied any resurrection — now come forward, fresh from the failure of their rivals, to try Christ with the riddle they had long used to ridicule the doctrine.

So the witnesses see the controversy pass to a third group with a different error. Where the Pharisees’ spies attacked on politics, the Sadducees attack on doctrine, denying the resurrection and the survival of the soul. The witnesses note the fathers’ characterisation: a sect proud of a righteousness they did not possess, materialist in belief, holding that death ends all. They come, Origen says, watching to entrap Christ, and bring out the conundrum with which they had often embarrassed the Pharisees. The verse thus opens the resurrection controversy, in which the Lord will not merely escape the trap but overthrow the whole Sadducean error, establishing the resurrection from the very books of Moses the Sadducees professed to honour. It marks the third wave of assault upon the teaching Christ, each from a different quarter, each to be turned to the questioners’ confusion and the truth’s confirmation.

So the witnesses see the controversy pass to a third group with a different error. Where the Pharisees’ spies attacked on politics, the Sadducees attack on doctrine, denying the resurrection and the survival of the soul. The witnesses note the fathers’ characterisation: a sect proud of a righteousness they did not possess, materialist in belief. They come, Origen says, watching to entrap Christ, with the conundrum they had often used against the Pharisees — which the Lord will overthrow from the very books of Moses they professed to honour.
28:
On Luke 20:28 — “Master, Moses wrote unto us, If any man’s brother die, having a wife, and he die without children… that his brother should take his wife, and raise up seed unto his brother” — the witnesses mark the appeal to the levirate law.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Wesley cite the source: Deuteronomy 25:5, the law of levirate marriage, by which a brother raised up offspring to one who died childless. Gill notes the substance, “though not in express words.”

The verse lays the foundation of the Sadducees’ riddle. They cite the Mosaic law of levirate marriage — that a man should take his dead brother’s widow to raise up seed in his name — as the premise on which they will build their case against the resurrection.

So the witnesses see the Sadducees construct their trap on Scripture they accept. The levirate law was a genuine ordinance of Moses, designed to preserve a dead man’s name and line in Israel; the Sadducees use it not for its purpose but as the scaffolding for a reductio against the resurrection. The witnesses note the cunning of grounding the riddle in the Pentateuch, the one part of Scripture the Sadducees fully owned, so that the difficulty might seem to arise from Moses himself. By starting from a law all granted, they hope to make the doctrine of the resurrection appear absurd — entangled in an insoluble tangle of remarriage. The verse thus sets the premise of their famous puzzle, and prepares the case of the seven brothers and one wife, by which they meant to laugh the resurrection out of court — not knowing that the Lord would answer them, in the end, from this same Moses.

So the witnesses see the Sadducees construct their trap on Scripture they accept. The levirate law was a genuine ordinance of Moses, designed to preserve a dead man’s name and line; the Sadducees use it not for its purpose but as scaffolding for a reductio against the resurrection. The witnesses note the cunning of grounding the riddle in the Pentateuch, the one part of Scripture the Sadducees fully owned, so the difficulty might seem to arise from Moses himself — not knowing the Lord would answer them, in the end, from this same Moses.
29:
On Luke 20:29 — “There were therefore seven brethren: and the first took a wife, and died without children” — the witnesses mark the construction of the puzzle.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Clarke refers to the parallel (Matthew 22:23). Gill notes the Sadducees “supposed such a case… and it might be fact.”

The verse begins the hypothetical case. Seven brothers in succession, under the levirate law, take the same woman to wife, and the first dies childless. The riddle is built brother by brother toward its intended absurdity.

So the witnesses watch the Sadducees lay out their contrived scenario. Whether a real case or, more likely, an invented one, it is designed to press the levirate law to an extreme that will make the resurrection seem ridiculous. The witnesses note the deliberate piling-up — seven brothers, one wife — each successive marriage lawful under Moses, each husband dying childless, so that the woman belongs in turn to all seven. The number is chosen for full effect, the case framed to maximise the supposed difficulty. The verse thus advances the Sadducees’ mocking puzzle: a tangle of seven lawful marriages that, they imagine, no doctrine of resurrection can unravel. It is the setup of a question asked not to learn but to ridicule — and the Lord’s answer will expose the false assumption on which the whole construction rests, that the life to come is merely a continuation of this one.

So the witnesses watch the Sadducees lay out their contrived scenario. Whether a real case or, more likely, invented, it is designed to press the levirate law to an extreme that will make the resurrection seem ridiculous. The witnesses note the deliberate piling-up — seven brothers, one wife — each successive marriage lawful under Moses, each husband dying childless. It is the setup of a question asked not to learn but to ridicule — and the Lord’s answer will expose the false assumption that the life to come merely continues this one. The verse thus shows the careful artifice of unbelief, which builds an elaborate case on a lawful ordinance only to mock the truth it dares not honestly examine.
30:
On Luke 20:30 — “And the second took her to wife, and he died childless” — the witnesses mark the puzzle’s repetition.

The voice here is the Reformed Gill, who notes the second brother “took her to wife, as he was obliged by the above law.”

The verse continues the case. The second brother, bound by the same levirate law, marries the widow, and he too dies childless. The pattern set in motion repeats toward the riddle’s climax.

So the witnesses follow the Sadducees’ deliberate repetition. Each brother in turn fulfils the law’s requirement, taking the childless widow; each dies without issue; and so the woman passes lawfully from one to the next. The witnesses note that the very monotony serves the trap’s purpose — to fix in the hearers’ minds the multiplied claim upon the one woman, so that the question “whose wife shall she be?” will seem unanswerable. The repetition assumes throughout that the bonds of this life carry unchanged into the next, the false premise on which the whole riddle depends. The verse thus marks the steady building of the Sadducees’ case, brother by brother, each marriage lawful under Moses, toward the moment when they will spring their question — and toward the Lord’s reply, which will overturn not their arithmetic but their fundamental misconception of the resurrection life.

So the witnesses follow the Sadducees’ deliberate repetition. Each brother in turn fulfils the law’s requirement, taking the childless widow; each dies without issue; so the woman passes lawfully from one to the next. The witnesses note that the very monotony serves the trap’s purpose — to fix in the hearers’ minds the multiplied claim upon one woman, so that “whose wife shall she be?” will seem unanswerable. The repetition assumes throughout that the bonds of this life carry unchanged into the next — the false premise on which the whole riddle depends. The verse thus shows how unbelief, having fixed on a false premise, multiplies its supposed difficulty by sheer repetition, mistaking the persistence of error for the weight of argument. The witnesses mark that the Lord will meet this accumulated error not by disputing its arithmetic but by overturning the assumption beneath it, that the risen life is bound by the marriages of the present age.
31:
On Luke 20:31 — “And the third took her; and in like manner the seven also: and they left no children, and died” — the witnesses mark the case completed.

The voice here is the Reformed Gill, who notes the third brother “took her to wife, by virtue of the same law,” and so through all seven.

The verse completes the chain. The third brother and all seven in turn marry the widow, and each dies childless. The full extent of the case is now before the hearers: one woman, lawfully wife to seven men.

So the witnesses see the Sadducees bring their construction to its head. All seven brothers, one after another, have taken the same woman under the levirate law, and all have died without children; the multiplied marriage is complete. The witnesses note that the deliberate fullness — not two or three but all seven — is meant to make the coming question as pointed as possible, the woman’s claim spread equally among all. The whole edifice rests, as before, on the unexamined assumption that marriage and its ties persist beyond the grave. The verse thus finishes the setup of the riddle: a tangle of seven lawful unions that, the Sadducees suppose, exposes the resurrection as incoherent. The stage is set for their triumphant question — and for the Lord’s answer, which will not solve the tangle on their terms but dissolve it, by showing that the risen life is not bound by the conditions of marriage and mortality at all.

So the witnesses see the Sadducees bring their construction to its head. All seven brothers, one after another, have taken the same woman under the levirate law, and all have died childless; the multiplied marriage is complete. The witnesses note that the deliberate fullness — not two or three but all seven — is meant to make the coming question as pointed as possible. The whole edifice rests on the unexamined assumption that marriage persists beyond the grave — which the Lord will not solve on their terms but dissolve, by showing the risen life unbound by marriage and mortality.
32:
On Luke 20:32 — “Last of all the woman died also” — the witnesses mark the close of the case.

The voice here is the Reformed Gill: “last of all the woman died also, having had no children by either of her seven husbands.”

The verse ends the hypothetical. The woman, having survived all seven husbands and borne none of them children, at last dies herself. The whole party of the case — seven brothers and one wife — has now passed into death, and the Sadducees are ready to pose their question.

So the witnesses note the tidy completion of the riddle. With the woman’s death the full company stands on the far side of the grave, and the Sadducees’ puzzle is ready to be sprung: if there is a resurrection, to which of the seven will she be restored as wife? The witnesses observe that the very neatness of the construction betrays its purpose — not honest inquiry but a contrived ridicule, built to make the doctrine of the resurrection collapse under the weight of an impossible domestic tangle. The childlessness, carefully noted, removes any way of distinguishing the husbands by offspring, so that all seven claims stand equal. The verse thus sets the last stone of the Sadducees’ argument in place, and prepares their question — the question that, far from confounding Christ, will give him the occasion to reveal both the nature of the resurrection life and the certainty of the resurrection itself, from the very Scriptures they professed to revere.

So the witnesses note the tidy completion of the riddle. With the woman’s death the full company stands on the far side of the grave, and the puzzle is ready to be sprung. The witnesses observe that the very neatness of the construction betrays its purpose — not honest inquiry but contrived ridicule, built to make the resurrection collapse under an impossible domestic tangle. The childlessness, carefully noted, removes any way of distinguishing the husbands by offspring — preparing the question that will give the Lord occasion to reveal both the nature and the certainty of the resurrection.
33:
On Luke 20:33 — “Therefore in the resurrection whose wife of them is she? for seven had her to wife” — the witnesses mark the Sadducees’ triumphant question.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the constative aorist “had her” (eschon), “including all seven” (Matthew 22:28). Gill frames the supposition: “at the time of the resurrection… supposing there will be such an one, which they denied.”

The verse springs the riddle. The Sadducees demand: in the resurrection, whose wife will she be, since all seven had her? The question is asked in mockery, certain there can be no answer, and so meant to expose the resurrection as absurd.

So the witnesses see the trap fully sprung. The Sadducees pose what they take to be an unanswerable question, confident that the impossibility of assigning the woman to one of seven husbands proves there can be no resurrection at all. The witnesses note the false assumption buried in the question — that the risen life simply prolongs the relations of this one, with its marriages and rivalries intact. It is on this assumption, not on any real difficulty, that the whole riddle stands; and it is precisely this that the Lord will overturn. The verse thus brings the Sadducees’ challenge to its point: a question designed to ridicule, springing from a carnal and mistaken notion of the world to come. The Lord’s reply will expose the error at its root — that they understand neither the Scriptures nor the power of God — and turn their mockery into the occasion of one of his clearest teachings on the resurrection.

So the witnesses see the trap fully sprung. The Sadducees pose what they take to be an unanswerable question, confident that the impossibility of assigning the woman to one of seven husbands proves there can be no resurrection. The witnesses note the false assumption buried in it — that the risen life simply prolongs this one, with its marriages and rivalries intact. It is on this, not on any real difficulty, that the whole riddle stands; and it is precisely this that the Lord will overturn, exposing the error at its root — that they understand neither the Scriptures nor the power of God.
34:
On Luke 20:34 — “And Jesus answering said unto them, The children of this world marry, and are given in marriage” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s distinction of the two ages.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrast peculiar to Luke. Ellicott: the contrast between “the children of this world” or age and those of “that world” is “peculiar to St. Luke” (cf. Luke 16:8). Clarke: “men and women in their present state of mortality… procreation being necessary to restore the waste made by death.” Wesley: they marry “as being all subject to the law of mortality.”

The verse begins the Lord’s answer by drawing the line the Sadducees had missed. Marriage belongs to “this world” — the present age of mortality, where the race must be renewed against the inroads of death. The whole riddle assumed that this condition continues; the Lord denies the assumption.

So the witnesses see the Lord strike at the root of the Sadducees’ error. Their puzzle rested on the supposition that the world to come is merely this world prolonged, with its marriages intact; the Lord answers by distinguishing the two ages. Marriage, the witnesses note with the fathers and expositors, is a provision of this world, bound up with mortality — men marry and are given in marriage because death must be repaired and the race continued. It is an institution for a dying world. The verse thus prepares the decisive correction: that in the age to come, where death is no more, the reason for marriage ceases, and with it the whole force of the Sadducees’ question. By naming the children of “this world” as those who marry, the Lord already implies that the children of “that world” do not — and so begins to dissolve the riddle by exposing the false premise on which it was built.

So the witnesses see the Lord strike at the root of the Sadducees’ error. Their puzzle rested on the supposition that the world to come is merely this world prolonged, with its marriages intact; the Lord answers by distinguishing the two ages. Marriage, the witnesses note with the fathers and expositors, is a provision of this world, bound up with mortality — men marry because death must be repaired and the race continued. By naming the children of “this world” as those who marry, the Lord already implies that the children of “that world” do not.
35:
On Luke 20:35 — “But they which shall be accounted worthy to obtain that world, and the resurrection from the dead, neither marry, nor are given in marriage” — the witnesses mark the change in the risen state.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the language. Ellicott: “accounted worthy” is “another word common to St. Luke and St. Paul” (2 Thessalonians 1:5). Gill is careful that this worthiness is “not by their own works and merits, but through the… righteousness of the Messiah.” Barnes points to those “accounted worthy” (Revelation 3:4).

The verse gives the heart of the answer. Those counted worthy of the world to come and the resurrection neither marry nor are given in marriage. The institution that belongs to this dying age has no place in the deathless one; and so the Sadducees’ question simply falls away.

So the witnesses see the Lord dissolve the riddle by revealing the nature of the risen life. The Sadducees had assumed marriage continues; Christ declares it does not. In “that world,” the witnesses note, the relations of this one are transcended — not the persons lost, but the condition changed, for marriage’s purpose, the renewing of a mortal race, is gone where none can die. The witnesses mark Gill’s careful note that to be “accounted worthy” is not by merit but through the righteousness of Christ — the resurrection to glory being the portion of the redeemed. The verse thus answers “whose wife shall she be?” not by choosing among the seven but by removing the question: in the resurrection she is wife to none, for marriage itself has passed away. The Lord’s reply lifts the hearers from the carnal puzzle to the true and higher character of the life to come.

So the witnesses see the Lord dissolve the riddle by revealing the nature of the risen life. The Sadducees had assumed marriage continues; Christ declares it does not. In “that world,” the relations of this one are transcended — not the persons lost, but the condition changed, for marriage’s purpose is gone where none can die. The witnesses mark Gill’s careful note that to be “accounted worthy” is not by merit but through Christ’s righteousness. The Lord answers “whose wife?” not by choosing among the seven but by removing the question.
36:
On Luke 20:36 — “Neither can they die any more: for they are equal unto the angels; and are the children of God, being the children of the resurrection” — the witnesses mark the deathless, angelic life of the risen.

The grammatical-historical commentators unfold the reasons. Robertson: “equal unto the angels” (isangeloi, a rare word, only here), and the Hebraistic “sons of the resurrection.” Ellicott marks the “implied thought… that death and marriage are correlative facts” — remove death, and marriage’s purpose ceases. Clarke and Barnes set it among the glories of the risen (1 Corinthians 15:42; Romans 8:17).

The verse gives the ground of the change. The risen cannot die any more; therefore, being beyond death, they need no marriage to repair the race, and are “equal unto the angels.” As children of the resurrection they are openly children of God. Immortality, not marriage, is the mark of that world.

So the witnesses see the Lord ground his answer in the deathlessness of the risen life. The whole reason for marriage — the renewing of a mortal race — vanishes where death itself is abolished; the risen, who “cannot die any more,” are therefore like the angels, who neither marry nor die. The witnesses note that this likeness to angels is not a loss of identity but an elevation: the redeemed become “sons of God,” manifestly his children in the glory of the resurrection. They mark Ellicott’s insight that death and marriage are correlative facts of this present order, so that the removal of the one ends the need of the other. The verse thus completes the Lord’s overthrow of the Sadducean riddle and unveils the splendour of the world to come: a deathless, angelic, filial life, where the conditions of mortality are left behind and the children of the resurrection live forever as the children of God.

So the witnesses see the Lord ground his answer in the deathlessness of the risen life. The whole reason for marriage — the renewing of a mortal race — vanishes where death itself is abolished; the risen, who cannot die, are like the angels, who neither marry nor die. The witnesses note that this likeness to angels is not a loss of identity but an elevation: the redeemed become “sons of God,” manifestly his children in the glory of the resurrection — a deathless, angelic, filial life where the conditions of mortality are left behind.
37:
On Luke 20:37 — “Now that the dead are raised, even Moses shewed at the bush, when he calleth the Lord the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob” — the witnesses mark the proof of the resurrection from Moses.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the skilful argument. Robertson: “Moses was used by the Sadducees…; this passage (Exodus 3:6) Jesus skilfully uses as a proof of the resurrection.” Ellicott: “shewed” means “indicating, pointing to.” The Reformed Calvin: having refuted the objection, Christ “confirms, by the testimony of Scripture, the doctrine of the final resurrection” — the order to be observed, repelling error then establishing truth.

The verse turns from refuting the riddle to proving the doctrine, and from the Sadducees’ own ground. Even Moses, at the burning bush, shows the dead are raised, for God there calls himself the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — long after the patriarchs had died.

So the witnesses admire the Lord’s choice of proof. The Sadducees revered Moses above all and used him against the resurrection; Christ answers from Moses, from the bush they could not gainsay. The witnesses note the precision: God does not say “I was” but “I am” the God of the patriarchs — and that, long after Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob had died in the body. The argument, drawn from the very Pentateuch the Sadducees owned, establishes that the patriarchs still live to God, and therefore shall be raised. The witnesses mark Calvin’s observation on method: first the objection is repelled, then the truth confirmed from Scripture, that the adversaries may know they oppose the word of God. The verse thus shows the Lord not only escaping the trap but turning the Sadducees’ authority against their error, founding the resurrection on the unimpeachable testimony of Moses himself.

So the witnesses admire the Lord’s choice of proof. The Sadducees revered Moses above all and used him against the resurrection; Christ answers from Moses, from the bush they could not gainsay. The witnesses note the precision: God says not “I was” but “I am” the God of the patriarchs — long after they had died. The argument, drawn from the Pentateuch the Sadducees owned, establishes that the patriarchs still live to God. Calvin marks the method: first repel the objection, then confirm the truth from Scripture, that adversaries know they oppose God’s word.
38:
On Luke 20:38 — “For he is not a God of the dead, but of the living: for all live unto him” — the witnesses mark the principle that clinches the proof.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the added clause. Ellicott: “all live unto him” is added by Luke alone, of value “as developing the meaning” — “all life… depends upon our relation to God.” Clarke cites Maccabean witness that the righteous “live unto God.” The Reformed Calvin: believers “after they have died in this world, lead a heavenly life with God” (Romans 14:8). Wesley: the term “God” implies a relation that “cannot possibly subsist between him and the dead” who are extinguished.

The verse states the principle on which the proof rests. God is the God of the living, not of the perished; if he is still the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, then they live to him — for to all who have him as their God, all are alive. The covenant relation guarantees that the patriarchs are not extinct.

So the witnesses see the argument completed and deepened. The force lies in what “God of” means: God does not style himself the God of the non-existent. If, ages after their death, he names himself the God of the patriarchs, they must live to him still; and what lives to God shall be raised. The witnesses note Luke’s added “all live unto him,” which widens the truth — the dead in Christ are not annihilated but alive in his presence, the soul surviving and the body destined to rise. Against the Sadducean denial of any life beyond death, the Lord asserts that the covenant God sustains his people in being forever. The verse thus founds the resurrection on the very nature of God’s relation to his own: he is the God of the living, and those whose God he is can never finally die, but live to him until the day he raises them whole.

So the witnesses see the argument completed and deepened. The force lies in what “God of” means: God does not style himself the God of the non-existent. If, ages after their death, he names himself the God of the patriarchs, they must live to him still; and what lives to God shall be raised. The witnesses note Luke’s added “all live unto him,” which widens the truth — the dead in Christ are not annihilated but alive in his presence. The covenant God sustains his people in being forever, until the day he raises them whole.
39:
On Luke 20:39 — “Then certain of the scribes answering said, Master, thou hast well said” — the witnesses mark the scribes’ reluctant praise.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the speakers. Robertson: “Pharisees who greatly enjoyed this use by Jesus… against the position of the Sadducees… they praise the reply… hostile though they are to him.” Ellicott: the words came “from the better section of the Pharisees, who welcomed this new defence of the doctrine on which their faith rested.” The Reformed Calvin: this confession was “extorted, by a secret exercise of divine power,” from men ill-disposed toward him.

The verse records an unexpected commendation. Some of the scribes — Pharisees, who held the resurrection — approve the Lord’s answer: “Master, thou hast well said.” His overthrow of the Sadducees pleased even his enemies, who shared the doctrine he defended.

So the witnesses note the praise wrung from hostile lips. The scribes were no friends of Christ, yet his masterly defence of the resurrection — against their own rivals, the Sadducees — compelled their approval. The witnesses mark, with Calvin, that even this grudging confession was drawn out by a secret working of God, who can make adversaries own the truth. They observe the partisan satisfaction in it: the Pharisees rejoiced to see the Sadducees confounded on the point that divided the two sects. Yet the praise, however sincere on this head, did not soften their enmity toward Christ himself. The verse thus shows the Lord’s wisdom commanding acknowledgment even from his foes, and the doctrine of the resurrection vindicated so plainly that those who came to entrap are left to applaud. It also sets up the final exchange, in which the Lord, having silenced every assailant, will himself put the question that none could answer.

So the witnesses note the praise wrung from hostile lips. The scribes were no friends of Christ, yet his masterly defence of the resurrection — against their rivals the Sadducees — compelled their approval. The witnesses mark, with Calvin, that even this grudging confession was drawn out by a secret working of God. They observe the partisan satisfaction in it: the Pharisees rejoiced to see the Sadducees confounded. Yet the praise did not soften their enmity toward Christ — setting up the final exchange, in which the Lord will himself put the question none could answer.
40:
On Luke 20:40 — “And after that they durst not ask him any question at all” — the witnesses mark the silencing of all the questioners.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the completeness. Robertson: “the courage of Pharisees, Sadducees, Herodians vanished” (double negative, imperfect). Clarke: they “did not venture to ask any other question, for fear of being again confounded.” Ellicott notes Luke omits the scribe’s question on the great commandment recorded by Matthew and Mark. Gill: “neither the Pharisees, Sadducees, Scribes, nor Herodians” dared ask more.

The verse marks the end of the assaults. After the Lord’s answers, none of his adversaries — of any party — dared question him further. The series of traps is over, every one defeated; the questioners are reduced to silence.

So the witnesses see the whole campaign against the teaching Christ collapse. Party after party — the Sanhedrin on authority, the Pharisees’ spies on tribute, the Sadducees on resurrection — had come to entrap him, and each had been turned back confounded. Now, the witnesses note, they give up: no one ventures another question, for fear of fresh humiliation. The silence is total and telling — the official wisdom of Israel, in all its factions, owns itself outmatched. The witnesses mark that this defeat of question prepares the reversal to come: having silenced every challenger, the Lord will now take the initiative and pose his own question, on the Sonship and Lordship of the Messiah, which none of them could answer. The verse thus closes the day’s controversies with Christ wholly victorious, his enemies mute, and the way open for him to press the truth about himself that all their questioning had failed to overthrow.

So the witnesses see the whole campaign against the teaching Christ collapse. Party after party — the Sanhedrin on authority, the spies on tribute, the Sadducees on resurrection — had come to entrap him, and each had been turned back confounded. Now they give up: no one ventures another question, for fear of fresh humiliation. The silence is total and telling — the official wisdom of Israel owns itself outmatched. This defeat prepares the reversal: having silenced every challenger, the Lord will now take the initiative and pose his own question.
41:
On Luke 20:41 — “And he said unto them, How say they that Christ is David’s son?” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s own question on the Messiah.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read it as a veiled disclosure of his Godhead. Theophylact: “though our Lord was shortly about to enter on His Passion, He proclaims His own Godhead… neither incautiously nor boastfully, but with modesty,” by a question that leaves them to reason out the conclusion. Cyril applies it against those who “divide one son into two”: how is David’s son David’s Lord, “not by human lordship, but divine?” The grammatical-historical Robertson notes that “they say” refers to the scribes (Matthew 22:42; Mark 12:35).

The verse turns the tables. Having silenced his questioners, the Lord poses his own: how can the scribes call the Christ merely David’s son? The question, modestly framed, opens the deepest truth about the Messiah’s person.

So the witnesses see the Lord, now on the offensive, raise the question of who the Messiah truly is. The scribes confessed the Christ to be David’s son — true as far as it went, his human descent; but the Lord presses beyond it to the mystery they had missed. The fathers find here a veiled but real proclamation of his Godhead: that the Christ is not only David’s son after the flesh but David’s Lord, divine as well as human. The witnesses note Cyril’s application against later error — that the one Christ is both, not divided into two sons. The Lord frames it as a question, modestly, leaving the hearers to draw the conclusion; but the conclusion is momentous. The verse thus turns the day’s controversies toward their true centre — the person of Christ — and prepares the proof from David’s own psalm that the Messiah is more than a son of David: he is his Lord.

So the witnesses see the Lord, now on the offensive, raise the question of who the Messiah truly is. The scribes confessed the Christ to be David’s son — true as far as it went, his human descent; but the Lord presses beyond it to the mystery they had missed. The fathers find here a veiled but real proclamation of his Godhead: that the Christ is not only David’s son after the flesh but David’s Lord, divine as well as human. Cyril applies it against later error — that the one Christ is both, not divided into two sons.
42:
On Luke 20:42 — “And David himself saith in the book of Psalms, The Lord said unto my Lord, Sit thou on my right hand” — the witnesses mark the proof from Psalm 110.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the authorship and force. Robertson: the language “clearly means that he treats David as the author of Psalm 110:1,” its inspiration and Messianic character affirmed in all three Synoptics (Acts 2:34). Lightfoot notes that the Talmudists once applied the psalm to the Messiah, before later evasions; “no man was able to answer him a word.” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem: “the Lord says this to the Lord, not to a servant, but to the Lord of all, and His own Son.”

The verse brings the proof. David, by inspiration, calls the Messiah “my Lord” — the one whom the LORD bids sit at his right hand. The psalm sets the Christ above David himself, at the throne of God.

So the witnesses see the Lord found his question on David’s own words. In Psalm 110:1, David — the supposed mere ancestor of the Christ — addresses the Christ as “my Lord,” and reports the LORD inviting him to sit at his right hand, the place of divine honour and rule. The witnesses note that all three Gospels treat David as the inspired author, and the psalm as Messianic, so that the testimony is David’s own and unimpeachable. Cyril of Jerusalem marks the weight of it: the words are spoken by the Lord to the Lord, to the Son and Lord of all, not to a servant. The verse thus lays the Scriptural ground for the Lord’s argument: that the Messiah, far from being merely David’s son, is David’s Lord, exalted to God’s right hand — a dignity no mere descendant could claim, and one that points to the Christ’s divine and eternal nature.

So the witnesses see the Lord found his question on David’s own words. In Ps 110:1, David — the supposed mere ancestor of the Christ — addresses the Christ as “my Lord,” and reports the LORD inviting him to sit at his right hand, the place of divine honour. The witnesses note that all three Gospels treat David as the inspired author, and the psalm as Messianic, so the testimony is David’s own and unimpeachable. Cyril of Jerusalem marks its weight: the words are spoken by the Lord to the Lord, to the Son and Lord of all.
43:
On Luke 20:43 — “Till I make thine enemies thy footstool” — the witnesses mark the promised subjugation of the Messiah’s foes.

The voices here are brief. The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke: “the footstool of thy feet” means the enemies “shall be actually subjected, and put completely under that Christ whom they now despise, and are about to crucify.” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem joins it to the Son’s session at the right hand of power. Gill notes it continues the citation from the psalm.

The verse completes the quotation. The LORD promises the exalted Christ that his enemies shall be made his footstool — wholly subdued beneath him. The Messiah enthroned at God’s right hand will see all opposition crushed under his feet.

So the witnesses read the climax of the psalm’s testimony. The Christ whom David calls Lord is not only seated at God’s right hand but assured the total conquest of his foes. The witnesses note the pointed application Clarke draws: the very enemies now plotting Christ’s death — the rulers who despise him — are among those who shall be put completely under his feet. The image of the footstool bespeaks utter subjugation, the conquered placed beneath the conqueror’s feet. The verse thus deepens the dignity the Lord is pressing: the Messiah is no mere son of David, no figure his enemies can overthrow, but the Lord enthroned beside God, whose triumph over all opposition is divinely guaranteed. Even as they prepare to crucify him, the rulers stand within the reach of this prophecy, which promises that the rejected stone, the slain heir, the Lord at God’s right hand, shall reign until every foe is subdued.

So the witnesses read the climax of the psalm’s testimony. The Christ whom David calls Lord is not only seated at God’s right hand but assured the total conquest of his foes. The witnesses note the pointed application Clarke draws: the very enemies now plotting Christ’s death are among those who shall be put under his feet. The image of the footstool bespeaks utter subjugation. Even as they prepare to crucify him, the rulers stand within reach of this prophecy, which promises that the rejected stone, the slain heir, shall reign until every foe is subdued.
44:
On Luke 20:44 — “David therefore calleth him Lord, how is he then his son?” — the witnesses mark the question that none could answer.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “on the basis of this definite piece of exegesis… Jesus presses the problem… for an explanation. The deity and the humanity of the Messiah in Psalm 110:1 are thus set forth.” Gill notes the inference: “David therefore called him Lord… upon which the following question is asked.”

The verse drives home the riddle. If David, by the Spirit, calls the Christ “Lord,” how can the Christ be merely David’s son? The two facts — David’s son and David’s Lord — can be reconciled only in one whose person is both human and divine.

So the witnesses see the Lord press the question to its point. He has shown that David calls the Messiah “Lord”; now he asks how that can be, if the Messiah is no more than David’s descendant. The witnesses note that the question is not a denial of the Messiah’s Davidic descent — which Scripture affirms — but a demand that the scribes reckon with the other half of the truth they had ignored. The only answer, the witnesses observe, is the one the scribes would not give: that the Christ is at once David’s son according to the flesh and David’s Lord according to his divine nature — truly man and truly God. Robertson notes that the deity and humanity of the Messiah are both set forth in the psalm. The verse thus brings the day’s controversies to their climax in the person of Christ: the question that silenced the rulers is the very mystery of the incarnation, that the Son of David is also the Son of God and Lord of all.

So the witnesses see the Lord press the question to its point. He has shown that David calls the Messiah “Lord”; now he asks how that can be, if the Messiah is no more than David’s descendant. The witnesses note that this is not a denial of the Davidic descent — which Scripture affirms — but a demand that the scribes reckon with the other half of the truth. The only answer is the one they would not give: that the Christ is at once David’s son according to the flesh and David’s Lord according to his divine nature — truly man and truly God.
45:
On Luke 20:45 — “Then in the audience of all the people he said unto his disciples” — the witnesses mark the turn to warn against the scribes.

The Eastern fathers note the timing and aim. Chrysostom: “nothing is more powerful than to argue from the Prophets… when they were silent, He warns against them.” Cyril: the scribes’ passions were “the love of vainglory and the love of gain,” and the Lord warns the disciples to avoid these “hateful crimes.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “in the hearing of all the people” (genitive absolute) — “that is the time to speak” (Mark 12:38).

The verse turns from the silenced adversaries to a public warning. Having confounded the scribes, the Lord now speaks to his disciples — but in the hearing of all the people — about the character of those scribes. The teaching is for the disciples; the exposure is for all.

So the witnesses see the Lord pass from controversy to caution. The scribes, defeated in argument, are now unmasked in character. The witnesses note that the warning is addressed to the disciples yet spoken openly, “in the audience of all the people” — the right moment, Robertson observes, to speak, when the scribes’ pretensions had just been silenced. Chrysostom marks the sequence: having stopped their mouths by the Prophets, the Lord now warns against them; Cyril names the besetting sins he will expose — vainglory and greed. The verse thus opens the final section of the chapter, in which the Lord, having vindicated the truth against every assault, turns to guard his followers against the hypocrisy of the religious leaders. The public setting ensures that the people, who had revered the scribes, hear their true character; and the disciples are taught, by the warning, what manner of men they must not become.

So the witnesses see the Lord pass from controversy to caution. The scribes, defeated in argument, are now unmasked in character. The witnesses note that the warning is addressed to the disciples yet spoken openly, “in the audience of all the people” — the right moment, Robertson observes, to speak. Chrysostom marks the sequence: having stopped their mouths by the Prophets, the Lord now warns against them; Cyril names the besetting sins he will expose — vainglory and greed. The people who had revered the scribes hear their true character.
46:
On Luke 20:46 — “Beware of the scribes, which desire to walk in long robes, and love greetings in the markets, and the highest seats… and the chief rooms at feasts” — the witnesses mark the catalogue of scribal pride.

The grammatical-historical commentators detail the vanities. Lightfoot describes the “long robes” of the disciples of the wise, reaching to the feet. Clarke: “take heed that ye be not seduced by those who should show you the way of salvation” (Matthew 23:5). Barnes points to the same love of “greetings” (Luke 11:43). The Eastern Cyril and Theophylact name the root — the courting of “a good reputation” and the hunt for honour and gain.

The verse exposes the scribes’ love of display. They covet the long robes that mark their rank, the public salutations, the chief seats in synagogue and at feasts — all the outward tokens of honour. Their religion has become a parade of self-importance.

So the witnesses see the Lord lay bare the vainglory of the religious leaders. Every item in the catalogue — the flowing robes, the marketplace greetings, the front seats, the places of honour at table — bespeaks a heart in love with the esteem of men. The witnesses note the fathers’ diagnosis: this is the love of vainglory, the courting of reputation, a religion turned into a stage for self. Clarke draws the warning that those who should guide others to salvation may instead seduce them by their example. The verse thus holds up the scribes as a warning to the disciples and to all: that the outward forms of piety may cloak an inward pride, and that the love of pre-eminence is the besetting sin of those who make religion a means of honour. It prepares the still graver charge of the next verse — that this pride is joined to oppression and greed, and shall meet a heavier judgment.

47:
On Luke 20:47 — “Which devour widows’ houses, and for a shew make long prayers: the same shall receive greater damnation” — the witnesses mark the scribes’ greed, hypocrisy, and doom.

The voices here are brief but pointed. The Reformed Gill: this “shows their avarice; they were very voracious and cruel; they did not spare widows, but devoured their substance.” The grammatical-historical Barnes ranges the charges and the sentence: devouring widows’ houses (Matthew 23:14), the “shew” of long prayers (1 Thessalonians 2:5), and the “greater damnation” (James 3:1).

The verse completes the indictment with the gravest charges. Beneath the show of piety the scribes are predators — devouring the houses of widows, the most defenceless — and they cloak their greed with long prayers made for display. For this they shall receive the heavier judgment.

So the witnesses see the scribes’ vainglory unmasked as something far darker. Their pride is joined to avarice and oppression: they prey on widows, consuming the substance of those whom God especially protects, and they gild their cruelty with ostentatious devotion. The witnesses note the hypocrisy the Lord exposes — long prayers made “for a shew,” religion turned into a cover for greed. And they mark the solemn sentence: “greater damnation,” the heavier condemnation reserved for those who sin under the cloak of sanctity and abuse a sacred office. The verse thus closes the chapter, and the Lord’s temple-teaching, on a note of severe warning: that the outward show of piety, when it masks oppression and self-seeking, does not lessen but aggravates guilt. The leaders who had spent the day trying to entrap Christ are left exposed in their true character, and warned of the stricter judgment their hypocrisy will meet.
